_comment: 1) Prefer events whose relevantOrigins match the individual's birth country/region.
2) Then prefer events whose continent matches the individual's inferred continent.
3) Always include up to 2 events where region === 'Global' if available.
4) Prefer events where the individual was age 10–50.
5) Never show more than 5 events per individual.

events.description: The Roman Empire shaped law, roads, cities, and trade around the Mediterranean. Its influence continued long after political power changed.
events.funFact: Roman concrete can still survive underwater for thousands of years—some modern concrete can’t match it.

events.event: Fall of the Western Roman Empire
events.description: Western Roman political authority collapsed, and new kingdoms formed across Europe. Local rulers and regional identities grew stronger.
events.funFact: Even after 476, people still called themselves “Roman” in some places for generations—like a long-lasting team name.

events.description: Islam spread rapidly across the Middle East, North Africa, and parts of Europe and Asia. New trade routes and learning centers flourished.
events.funFact: Scholars in this era preserved and expanded math and astronomy—ideas that later helped modern science grow.

events.description: Seafarers from Scandinavia traded, explored, and raided across Europe and beyond. Their voyages connected the North Atlantic and river routes into Eastern Europe.
events.funFact: Vikings reached North America around the year 1000—about 500 years before Columbus.

events.description: Charlemagne ruled much of Western Europe and promoted education and record-keeping. His era shaped later European borders and institutions.
events.funFact: Many books survived only because scribes copied them by hand—one mistake could change a sentence forever.

events.description: China’s Song era saw major advances in technology, cities, and trade. Printing, navigation, and commerce expanded dramatically.
events.funFact: Paper money was used in China centuries before it became common in Europe.

events.description: Religious and political wars increased contact between Europe and the Middle East. Trade, travel, and ideas moved between regions.
events.funFact: Spices like pepper became so valuable that they were sometimes stored like treasure.

events.description: The Mongol Empire expanded across Asia and into Europe, linking trade routes and moving people and goods across continents.
events.funFact: Horse relay stations let messages travel huge distances—like an ancient express-mail network.

events.description: A devastating pandemic spread across Europe, North Africa, and parts of Asia, killing millions. It changed labor, cities, and family life for generations.
events.funFact: Some towns created early quarantine rules—ships could be forced to wait offshore before anyone could land.

events.description: Large Chinese fleets traveled through Southeast Asia, South Asia, and East Africa. These voyages show how connected the world already was by sea.
events.funFact: Some ships were so large they were described like floating cities—many times bigger than typical European ships of the time.

events.description: The Ottoman Empire captured Constantinople, shifting power and trade between Europe and Asia. The city later became Istanbul.
events.funFact: Huge cannons were rolled into place on logs—like moving a house-sized machine without engines.

events.description: Europe and the Americas became permanently connected, launching centuries of migration, trade, and conflict.
events.funFact: Columbus thought he reached Asia—so the “West Indies” got a name based on a wrong guess.

events.description: Religious reform movements reshaped European politics and community life. Conflicts and migrations followed as regions adopted different faiths.
events.funFact: Printed pamphlets spread ideas fast—like the viral posts of the 1500s.

events.event: Fall of the Aztec capital Tenochtitlan
events.description: Spanish forces and Indigenous allies captured the Aztec capital, dramatically changing power in central Mexico. Colonial rule reshaped societies and labor.
events.funFact: Tenochtitlan had canals and causeways—parts of it looked more like a water city than a typical European town.

events.event: Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire
events.description: Spanish forces captured the Inca ruler and dismantled the empire’s leadership. The Andes region underwent major demographic change.
events.funFact: The Inca used knotted strings called quipu to record information—like a woven spreadsheet.

events.description: Huge silver mining in the Andes fueled global trade and empire finances. It linked the Americas, Europe, Africa, and Asia through money and goods.
events.funFact: So much silver came from one mountain that “worth a Potosí” became a saying for unbelievable riches.

events.description: A Spanish fleet failed to invade England, shifting naval power and European politics. Sea power and trade routes became even more important.
events.funFact: Storms wrecked many ships—some sailors joked the weather fought harder than cannons.

events.description: Forced migration of African people to the Americas grew into a vast system tied to plantations and global trade. Its effects shaped families and societies for centuries.
events.funFact: Ships were designed to pack people tightly—an inhuman “math problem” meant to maximize profit.

events.description: England’s first permanent settlement in North America was established in Virginia. Survival depended on farming, trade, and uneasy relations with neighbors.
events.funFact: The site was good for ships but bad for health—marshy water meant mosquitoes thrived.

events.description: A major conflict in Central Europe reshaped borders and disrupted daily life. It affected German-speaking lands and neighboring regions deeply.
events.funFact: Some towns lost more than half their people—whole villages had to be rebuilt from scratch.

events.description: English settlers landed near Plymouth and signed an early agreement for self-government. It influenced later colonial governance traditions.
events.funFact: The Mayflower spent 66 days at sea—longer than many summer vacations today.

events.description: The Mughal Empire governed large parts of South Asia, supporting art, architecture, and trade. Cities and agriculture expanded under imperial administration.
events.funFact: Mughal artisans made textiles so fine that some cloth was called “woven air.”

events.description: Power shifted from the Ming to the Qing dynasty, changing administration and society. Warfare and instability affected migration.
events.funFact: A hairstyle (the queue) became political—hair could signal loyalty or rebellion.

events.description: Conflicts in England over monarchy and Parliament affected politics and trade across the Atlantic world.
events.funFact: Pamphlets and rumors crossed the ocean—sometimes arriving before ships carrying official news.

events.event: Dutch Golden Age trade and science
events.description: The Dutch Republic became a major trading and scientific power. Global shipping networks connected Europe to Asia and the Americas.
events.funFact: Dutch merchants used wind-powered sawmills to cut timber faster—like an early factory on the water’s edge.

events.description: A massive fire destroyed much of London, leading to rebuilding and new ideas about city safety.
events.funFact: People escaped by boat on the Thames—like a floating emergency exit.

events.description: Accusations of witchcraft led to trials and executions in colonial Massachusetts. Fear and rumor shaped outcomes more than evidence.
events.funFact: Some “tests” used were based on superstition—like judging guilt by strange coincidences.

events.description: Machines and factories transformed manufacturing and urban life. Work shifted from home workshops to mills and industrial towns.
events.funFact: One factory machine could replace many hand spinners—like switching from walking to a bike overnight.

events.description: A wave of religious revivals reshaped communities across the American colonies. It influenced politics and culture as well as faith.
events.funFact: Some revival crowds were so big that people climbed trees just to see and hear.

events.event: French and Indian War / Seven Years’ War (North America)
events.description: Britain and France fought for control in North America, with many Indigenous nations involved. The war reshaped borders and colonial politics.
events.funFact: Control of one river could decide where whole towns grew—rivers were the highways of the 1700s.

events.description: A war for independence created the United States and changed global politics. It affected families through shortages, military service, and shifting loyalties.
events.funFact: A musket could take 15–20 seconds to reload—imagine waiting that long between each “shot” in a game.

events.description: Political upheaval in France reshaped European politics and ideas about rights and citizenship. Wars and instability followed across the continent.
events.funFact: Revolutionaries even changed the calendar—trying to rename months and weeks like a total schedule reset.

events.description: A successful revolt ended slavery in Saint-Domingue and created Haiti. It influenced abolition debates across the Atlantic world.
events.funFact: Haiti became the first Black republic in the modern world—an achievement that shocked empires.

events.description: The U.S. bought a huge territory from France, doubling its size and accelerating westward migration.
events.funFact: The price worked out to about four cents per acre—cheaper than a sticker for a giant yard.

events.description: The U.S. and Britain fought over trade and power in North America. It influenced national identity and borders.
events.funFact: The White House was rebuilt after being burned—repainting made the “White House” nickname stick.

events.event: Trail of Tears and forced removals
events.description: U.S. policy forced many Indigenous nations from their homelands to territories west of the Mississippi. The removal journeys caused immense suffering.
events.funFact: Some groups were marched more than 1,000 miles—about the distance from Chicago to New Orleans.

events.description: Brazil became independent from Portugal, shaping politics and identity in South America. A large empire formed rather than many smaller states.
events.funFact: Brazil’s first leader after independence was a prince—like a royal “founder” of a new country.

events.event: Abolition of slavery in the British Empire
events.description: Britain outlawed slavery across its empire, reshaping labor and migration patterns. The change influenced abolition movements elsewhere.
events.funFact: Britain paid slave owners compensation—money that came from taxes and was only finally paid off in modern times.

events.description: Railroads, land laws, and economic booms drew people west. New towns formed while Indigenous peoples faced displacement.
events.funFact: A standard land claim was 160 acres—about 120 football fields side by side.

events.description: Southern states seceded and fighting began, turning political crisis into war. Families faced enlistment, shortages, and fear.
events.funFact: Many soldiers’ first “uniforms” were whatever clothes they already owned—real standard uniforms came later.

events.description: A U.S. wartime proclamation declared freedom for enslaved people in rebelling states and shifted the war’s meaning.
events.funFact: Some people learned the news through songs and church announcements before ever seeing a newspaper.

events.description: After the Civil War, the U.S. rebuilt and debated new definitions of citizenship and rights. Politics and violence shaped the era.
events.funFact: Amendments added after the war changed the Constitution—like major rule updates to a country’s playbook.

events.description: A U.S. law offered land to settlers who lived on it and improved it. It encouraged massive migration and new farms.
events.funFact: 160 acres is about 120 football fields—free land in exchange for five years of hard work.

events.description: A railroad linked U.S. coasts, transforming travel and trade. It accelerated western settlement and economic growth.
events.funFact: A trip that once took months could shrink to about a week—like turning a long hike into a train ride.

events.description: Rapid industrial growth created new fortunes and harsh working conditions. Cities expanded and labor movements grew.
events.funFact: Some factories used whistles to signal shifts—like a giant school bell for an entire neighborhood.

events.description: Millions of immigrants entered the U.S. through Ellis Island, reshaping cities and communities. Medical and legal inspections were part of arrival.
events.funFact: Inspectors watched people climb stairs to spot illness—one quick test before modern scanners.

events.description: A short war marked the U.S. becoming more involved in global affairs. It affected Cuba, Puerto Rico, and the Philippines.
events.funFact: Newspaper headlines helped shape public opinion quickly—like a pre-internet news frenzy.

events.description: Conflict followed U.S. control of the Philippines, involving guerrilla fighting and harsh conditions. It shaped debates about empire and independence.
events.funFact: Some fighters used hidden supply routes through forests and villages—like a real-life stealth strategy game.

events.description: A massive earthquake and fire devastated San Francisco and led to extensive rebuilding. It influenced building codes and city planning.
events.funFact: Some families cooked meals on sidewalks using makeshift stoves because kitchens were gone.

events.description: A factory fire in New York killed many workers and sparked labor and safety reforms. It became a symbol of workplace danger.
events.funFact: After the fire, laws changed so factory doors couldn’t be locked—safety rules were literally written in memory.

events.description: Global war reshaped borders and technology. U.S. entry in 1917 increased manpower and industrial production for the war effort.
events.funFact: Some soldiers used carrier pigeons to send messages—feathered “text messages” that saved lives.

events.description: A major European war involved Russia and an alliance including Britain and France. It influenced military medicine and reporting.
events.funFact: Newspapers sent war reports that people read at breakfast—like daily live updates without the internet.

events.description: Russia ended legal serfdom for millions, reshaping rural life and labor. The reform had limits but changed society significantly.
events.funFact: Some families suddenly needed new paperwork to prove their status—like a giant identity update for a whole country.

events.description: Canada formed a federal union, shaping politics and migration routes in North America. It strengthened national institutions.
events.funFact: Building a country meant arguing about railways and provinces—like assembling a huge team with many captains.

events.description: War between France and Prussia helped drive German unification and altered European power. It affected borders and national identity.
events.funFact: After the war, Paris famously used hot-air balloons to send mail over enemy lines—airmail before airplanes.

events.description: A multi-ethnic empire governed Central Europe, including Bohemia and other regions. National movements and tensions grew over time.
events.funFact: In one empire, official notices might appear in multiple languages—like a town bulletin board with many translations.

events.description: Crop failure and policies contributed to mass starvation and emigration. Families dispersed across the Atlantic world.
events.funFact: Ireland exported food during the famine—ships left ports daily while people nearby went hungry.

events.description: Political upheaval and conflict led to the Irish Free State and partition. It influenced migration and identity across Irish communities worldwide.
events.funFact: Secret messages and coded notes were used—like a real-life spy story in city streets.

events.description: Wars in Southeast Europe reshaped borders and heightened tensions before World War I. Civilian displacement increased.
events.funFact: Borders changed so fast that some families needed new passports even though they never moved houses.

events.description: Mass killings and deportations of Armenians occurred in the Ottoman Empire during World War I. The events remain a major historical tragedy.
events.funFact: Some survivors kept tiny family photographs as their most precious possessions during forced marches.

events.description: Revolution ended the Russian Empire and led to years of civil war and upheaval. It reshaped politics and migration across Eastern Europe.
events.funFact: Trains became moving lifelines—some people lived on railcars for weeks while cities changed hands.

events.description: Conflict between Britain and China led to unequal treaties and new trade terms. It shaped China’s relations with European powers.
events.funFact: Treaty ports became new “gateway cities” where languages and goods mixed like a giant international market.

events.description: Further conflict expanded foreign influence in China. It affected trade, politics, and internal stability.
events.funFact: Some officials relied on handwritten reports carried days by runners—news traveled at the speed of feet.

events.description: Japan rapidly modernized government, industry, and military power. The changes transformed education and daily life.
events.funFact: Japan built railways so fast that some people saw their first train before they ever saw a bicycle.

events.description: An anti-foreign uprising in China led to international intervention and further political turmoil. It influenced migration and reform movements.
events.funFact: Messages sometimes traveled by lantern signals and runners—like night-time Morse code without wires.

events.description: Britain governed much of the Indian subcontinent, reshaping economies, infrastructure, and politics. Resistance movements grew over time.
events.funFact: The railways in India became one of the largest networks on Earth—like a metal web connecting thousands of towns.

events.description: British rule ended and new nations formed, leading to massive migration and violence. Millions moved across new borders.
events.funFact: It was one of the largest migrations in history—so many people moved that trains were packed like giant moving cities.

events.event: Chinese Civil War and Communist Revolution
events.description: Civil war ended with the establishment of the People’s Republic of China. The conflict reshaped families, land, and migration.
events.funFact: Some families hid important papers in walls or jars—like real-life secret treasure to protect their future.

events.description: Korea experienced colonial rule and resistance movements, later leading into Cold War division. Families faced political pressure and cultural suppression.
events.funFact: Some activists printed secret newspapers—tiny pages passed hand-to-hand like forbidden comics.

events.description: The Philippines moved from Spanish to U.S. control and later toward independence. Political movements and conflicts shaped national identity.
events.funFact: Some rebels used hidden mountain paths like secret shortcuts only locals knew.

events.description: Australian colonies formed a federal commonwealth, shaping politics and identity in Oceania. Institutions and immigration policies developed.
events.funFact: Australia’s first parliament met in a building that used to be an exhibition hall—like starting a government in a giant fairground.

events.event: New Zealand grants women the vote
events.description: New Zealand became the first self-governing country to grant women the right to vote. The change influenced suffrage movements worldwide.
events.funFact: Petitions were so long that some were rolled like giant carpets when delivered to parliament.

events.event: World War II in the Pacific
events.description: Major battles across the Pacific involved Japan, the U.S., and many island territories. The war transformed societies and migration across the region.
events.funFact: Some island runways were built so quickly that coral was crushed into “instant concrete” for aircraft to land on.

events.description: European powers colonized and divided much of Africa, reshaping borders and economies. Local resistance and negotiation varied by region.
events.funFact: Some borders were drawn with rulers on maps—straight lines that ignored rivers, mountains, and communities.

events.description: Conflict between the British Empire and the Zulu Kingdom shaped Southern African history. Battles and diplomacy changed regional power.
events.funFact: At Isandlwana, Zulu forces defeated a British camp—an upset that surprised the world’s newspapers.

events.description: Ethiopia resisted colonization and defeated Italian forces, preserving independence. The victory became a symbol across Africa and beyond.
events.funFact: News of Adwa inspired people worldwide—proof that a European empire could be stopped.

events.description: War in Southern Africa involved the British Empire and Boer republics. It affected civilians and introduced controversial camps and tactics.
events.funFact: Some communications used heliographs—mirrors flashing sunlight like a laser message across miles.

events.description: Political movements pushed for Egyptian independence from Britain, leading to a new kingdom with continued foreign influence.
events.funFact: Crowds sometimes used songs and street theater to spread messages—like a musical news broadcast.

events.event: Ottoman dissolution and Republic of Turkey
events.description: After World War I, the Ottoman Empire collapsed and modern Turkey formed. Borders and identities shifted across the region.
events.funFact: New borders meant some families became “foreigners” without moving—just because the map changed.

events.description: A political movement advocating a Jewish homeland grew in Europe and the Middle East. Migration and international debate increased.
events.funFact: Some early settlers planted forests by hand—trees became symbols of rebuilding a home.

events.event: State of Israel declared and first Arab–Israeli war
events.description: Israel declared independence and war followed with neighboring states. The conflict created major refugee movements and lasting regional tensions.
events.funFact: Families sometimes carried keys to homes they left behind—hoping to return someday.

events.event: Oil discovered in Saudi Arabia (commercial era begins)
events.description: Large-scale oil development reshaped economies and global politics in the Middle East. New industries and cities grew rapidly.
events.funFact: Oil wealth helped build cities in deserts—places that grew from small towns to skyscrapers in one lifetime.

events.event: World War II North Africa campaign
events.description: Major battles in North Africa involved Axis and Allied forces, affecting Egypt, Libya, Tunisia, and Algeria. Control of supply routes was crucial.
events.funFact: Desert soldiers used compasses and stars—navigation could be as important as weapons.

events.description: Independence movements reshaped northern South America, creating new nations. Long campaigns and political debates followed.
events.funFact: Some armies crossed the Andes on foot—higher than many ski resorts and colder than people expected.

events.description: War between Mexico and the U.S. changed borders and politics in North America. It influenced westward migration and future conflicts.
events.funFact: Some troops marched hundreds of miles with heavy gear—like carrying a backpack every day for months.

events.description: A major revolution transformed Mexico’s politics, land rights, and society. Many people migrated internally and across borders.
events.funFact: Revolutionary leaders became folk heroes—songs and stories spread their names like famous athletes today.

events.event: Panama becomes independent; canal era begins
events.description: Panama separated from Colombia, and plans for a major canal accelerated. The canal later transformed global shipping routes.
events.funFact: A ship crossing the canal saves about 8,000 miles compared to sailing around South America.

events.description: Argentina received large waves of European immigrants, reshaping cities and farming regions. New communities formed around work and land.
events.funFact: Buenos Aires grew so fast that people joked it was “the Paris of South America,” full of new theaters and cafés.

events.description: Cuba fought for independence from Spain, leading toward major political changes and later U.S. involvement.
events.funFact: Newspapers followed the conflict closely—some people felt like they were watching a serialized drama in real time.

events.description: Sugar plantations and trade shaped Caribbean societies and global markets. Labor systems—including slavery and indenture—deeply affected families.
events.funFact: Sugar was once a luxury—some Europeans treated it like medicine or a rare spice.

events.description: Rubber demand drove rapid growth in parts of the Amazon, reshaping labor and cities. Exploitation and hardship were common.
events.funFact: Rubber helped power the bicycle and car revolution—tires made wheeled travel smoother and faster.

events.event: Banana company era in Central America
events.description: Large foreign companies controlled land and infrastructure in parts of Central America, shaping politics and labor. The term “banana republic” emerged from this era.
events.funFact: Some companies ran their own railroads and ports—like a private mini-country built around fruit.

events.description: War on the Korean Peninsula involved global powers and caused huge civilian hardship. It reinforced Cold War divisions.
events.funFact: Some families listened to radio updates daily—like following a sports season, but with real danger.

events.description: A Cold War confrontation brought the world close to nuclear war. Diplomacy and secrecy were crucial.
events.funFact: School kids practiced safety drills—imagine a “nuclear drill” the way we do fire drills.

events.description: A long conflict shaped politics and families around the world. Media coverage changed how people understood war.
events.funFact: Television brought war into living rooms—people could see events only days after they happened.

events.description: Political campaigns transformed education, culture, and daily life in China. Families faced upheaval and social pressure.
events.funFact: Some students carried “little red books” everywhere—like a pocket rulebook for what to say and do.

events.description: Revolution in Iran replaced the monarchy with an اسلامی republic, reshaping politics and society. It affected regional and global relations.
events.funFact: Crowds used cassette tapes to spread speeches—like sharing audio messages before the internet.

events.description: A brutal regime caused mass death and displacement in Cambodia. Families were separated and communities devastated.
events.funFact: Survivors later used hidden diaries and drawings to prove what happened—paper became evidence and memory.

events.description: A labor movement challenged communist rule and inspired reform across Eastern Europe. It helped set the stage for later political change.
events.funFact: Workers posted notices on shipyard gates—like a real-world bulletin board that changed a country.

events.description: Large protests in Beijing called for political reform and were violently suppressed. The events influenced global politics and memory.
events.funFact: Photographs from that year spread worldwide and became symbols—images can travel farther than any one person.

events.description: Mass violence in Rwanda killed hundreds of thousands in a short time. The tragedy reshaped families and regional politics.
events.funFact: Radio was used to spread messages—showing how powerful information can be, for good or for harm.

events.event: End of apartheid in South Africa
events.description: South Africa dismantled apartheid and held multiracial elections. The change reshaped rights, politics, and daily life.
events.funFact: For the first time, many adults voted in a free election—some said it felt like finally being counted.

events.description: European sea powers built long-distance trade routes linking the Americas, Africa, Europe, and Asia. New goods, people, and diseases moved between continents.
events.funFact: A ship could take months to cross oceans—imagine waiting all summer just for one letter.

events.description: The Ottoman Empire grew in the Middle East, North Africa, and parts of Europe, shaping trade and politics. Cities and caravan routes connected regions.
events.funFact: Istanbul sat at a crossroads—merchants could buy goods from three continents in one city.

events.description: A powerful Persian empire shaped culture, trade, and religion in the Middle East and Central Asia. Its cities became centers of art and learning.
events.funFact: Persian carpets were so prized that some families treated them like heirlooms passed down for generations.

events.description: Spain founded St. Augustine, one of the oldest European-established cities in what is now the United States. It became a strategic coastal fort town.
events.funFact: St. Augustine is older than Jamestown by more than 40 years—history in the U.S. has deeper layers than many people realize.

events.description: Japan entered a long period of relative peace and internal stability under the Tokugawa shoguns. Cities grew and culture flourished.
events.funFact: Edo (Tokyo) became one of the world’s largest cities—without modern cars or skyscrapers.

events.description: China’s population and agriculture expanded, with large cities and trade networks. This growth shaped migration and local economies.
events.funFact: Some Chinese cities had neighborhoods with specialized trades—entire streets known for one craft.

events.description: Russia founded a new capital city to face Europe, reshaping politics and trade. The city grew into a major cultural center.
events.funFact: St. Petersburg was built on marshy land—builders had to fight mud and water as much as they built walls.

events.event: War of Jenkins’ Ear / Caribbean conflicts
events.description: European empires fought for trade routes and colonies in the Atlantic world. Caribbean ports and shipping lanes were strategic targets.
events.funFact: A single captured ship could be worth a fortune—like winning a treasure chest at sea.

events.description: Campaigns against slavery expanded in Europe and the Americas, changing laws and public debates. The pace differed by region and economy.
events.funFact: Some abolitionists used powerful posters and stories—like early viral media meant to change hearts.

events.description: Haiti became independent after a successful revolution, reshaping Atlantic politics. It influenced debates about slavery and empire.
events.funFact: Haiti’s revolution forced empires to rethink the idea that colonies were “unbreakable.”

events.description: A massive civil war in China caused enormous loss of life and disruption. It affected cities, farms, and migration across regions.
events.funFact: It was one of the deadliest conflicts in human history—larger than many famous European wars combined.

events.event: Abolition of slavery in the United States (13th Amendment)
events.description: The U.S. ended legal slavery nationwide, reshaping law and society. Freedom expanded but equality remained contested.
events.funFact: An amendment is like a permanent rule change—written into the country’s core instruction manual.

events.description: Chile fought Peru and Bolivia, changing borders and control of resource-rich regions. The war influenced national politics for decades.
events.funFact: Battles were fought in deserts so dry that water logistics could decide who won.

events.description: Japan defeated China, shifting power in East Asia and influencing modernization and imperial ambitions.
events.funFact: The war helped show that an industrialized navy could change regional power fast—like a technology leap deciding the outcome.

events.description: Anti-foreign violence and international intervention deepened China’s political crises. The event influenced reforms and later revolutions.
events.funFact: Some messages were carried by runners at night—like human “push notifications” across a city.

events.description: Japan’s victory over Russia shocked the world and shifted power in East Asia. It influenced revolution and reform in both countries.
events.funFact: It was the first time in modern history an Asian power defeated a major European empire in a large war.

events.description: Trench warfare, new weapons, and mass mobilization transformed Europe. Borders and empires changed dramatically afterward.
events.funFact: Soldiers sometimes used periscopes to peek above trenches—like a submarine tool used on land.

events.description: A major peace treaty after World War I reshaped borders and imposed reparations. It influenced politics and future conflicts.
events.funFact: The treaty was signed in the Hall of Mirrors—an extravagant room where reflections made it feel like a thousand witnesses.

events.description: A new international organization aimed to prevent future wars. Its successes and failures shaped later global institutions.
events.funFact: The League was like an early attempt at a “world group chat” for diplomacy—sometimes helpful, sometimes ignored.

events.description: Alcohol was banned nationwide, changing culture and crime. Many people ignored the law, creating hidden markets.
events.funFact: Some speakeasies hid behind fake walls or bookshelves—like secret levels in a building.

events.description: Japan expanded into northeast China, escalating tensions in East Asia. The event helped set the stage for later war.
events.funFact: Railways and factories made the region strategically valuable—industry could be as important as armies.

events.description: A civil war in Spain drew international attention and tested new military tactics. It influenced politics across Europe.
events.funFact: Some photographers’ images became famous worldwide—proof that pictures can change how people feel about a war.

events.event: Atomic bombs end WWII in the Pacific
events.description: Nuclear weapons were used in Japan, and World War II ended soon after. The world entered the nuclear age.
events.funFact: The power came from splitting atoms—tiny building blocks so small you can’t see them even with a microscope.

events.description: Many colonies in Asia and Africa gained independence, reshaping global politics. New nations formed with new borders and challenges.
events.funFact: Some countries chose new flags and anthems in the same year—like designing a whole national “profile page.”

events.description: Leaders from Asia and Africa met to discuss cooperation and post-colonial futures. It influenced the Non-Aligned Movement.
events.funFact: It was like a giant meeting where newly independent countries compared notes on how to build their futures.

events.description: Oil supply shocks raised prices and changed economies worldwide. Energy policy and daily life shifted suddenly.
events.funFact: Some countries had gas lines so long that people brought books and snacks to wait like they were camping.

events.description: A nuclear accident spread contamination across parts of Europe. It affected health, politics, and public trust.
events.funFact: Scientists detected radioactive particles far away within days—like invisible dust that traveled on the wind.

events.description: Hong Kong transitioned from British to Chinese sovereignty, shaping politics and identity in the region.
events.funFact: A whole city’s flag and official stamps changed overnight—like switching an account’s settings instantly.

events.description: A Spanish expedition completed the first known voyage around the Earth, proving global sea travel was possible. It reshaped maps and trade ambitions.
events.funFact: The crew set out with 5 ships—but only 1 returned, showing how risky exploration was.

events.event: Cape Town founded as a supply station
events.description: A European resupply station at the Cape became a major port linking Atlantic and Indian Ocean routes. It later shaped migration and colonial politics in Southern Africa.
events.funFact: Ships stopped for fresh water and food—like an ocean gas station for the age of sail.

events.description: A French invasion of Egypt affected Middle Eastern politics and European rivalry. Scholars with the army documented ancient sites and languages.
events.funFact: The Rosetta Stone helped crack Egyptian hieroglyphs—like finding a secret decoder key.

events.description: Conflict in Afghanistan reflected rivalry between empires in Central and South Asia. The war affected regional politics and migration.
events.funFact: One retreat from Kabul became famous because so few soldiers made it out—history’s version of an impossible escape story.

events.description: A major uprising against British rule changed governance in India and intensified colonial control. It left deep impacts on communities and memory.
events.funFact: News traveled by runner, horse, and telegraph—sometimes arriving too late to stop a battle.

events.event: Abolition of slavery in Brazil begins (free womb law era later)
events.description: Brazil slowly moved toward ending slavery through a series of laws, culminating in abolition in 1888. The long process affected families and labor systems.
events.funFact: Brazil was the last country in the Americas to abolish slavery—ending it in 1888.

events.description: Brazil abolished slavery, transforming labor and society. The change reshaped family histories across the country.
events.funFact: The law abolishing slavery was only a few paragraphs long—short words with huge impact.

events.description: A canal connecting the Atlantic and Pacific transformed global shipping and travel. It changed trade routes and strategic power.
events.funFact: Ships ride giant water “elevators” (locks) that lift them up and down as they cross the isthmus.

events.description: Italy invaded Ethiopia, and Ethiopia resisted in a major anti-colonial conflict. The war influenced international politics and African resistance movements.
events.funFact: Ethiopia’s earlier victory at Adwa was remembered as a symbol that empires could be beaten.

events.description: Conflict ended with the establishment of the People’s Republic of China and major social changes. Migration and diaspora increased in some regions.
events.funFact: Some families hid important documents in jars or walls—paper became a survival tool.

events.description: The first artificial satellite sparked global competition in science and technology. Education and research funding surged.
events.funFact: Sputnik’s “beep” could be heard by radio operators—people listened to space like it was a new station.

events.description: Many African countries gained independence, reshaping politics and borders. New governments faced big challenges and opportunities.
events.funFact: In 1960 alone, 17 African countries became independent—like a whole continent getting many new birthdays at once.

events.description: Bangladesh became independent after conflict and humanitarian crisis. The event reshaped South Asian politics and migration.
events.funFact: Millions fled across borders—one of the largest refugee movements of the 20th century.

events.description: Student protests in South Africa challenged apartheid and drew global attention. The uprising became a turning point in resistance movements.
events.funFact: Schoolchildren became the loudest voices—young people helped force the world to pay attention.

events.event: Ethiopian famine and global aid response
events.description: Drought and conflict contributed to famine in Ethiopia, leading to major humanitarian responses. The crisis shaped global awareness of aid.
events.funFact: Live concerts raised money for relief—music became a way to send help across the world.

events.description: A long and costly war ended, affecting families across the region. Reconstruction and political changes followed.
events.funFact: The war lasted 8 years—longer than most kids’ entire elementary school time.

events.description: Conflict in the Balkans followed the breakup of Yugoslavia, causing displacement and tragedy. International intervention and peace accords followed.
events.funFact: Some cities survived long sieges where people used homemade stoves and collected rainwater to live.

events.description: A political agreement helped reduce violence in Northern Ireland and created new power-sharing structures. It marked a major step toward peace.
events.funFact: Peace talks lasted so long that negotiators joked the real challenge was staying awake—history sometimes turns on endurance.

events.description: Governments and companies prepared for possible computer date errors at the year 2000. The effort showed how dependent the world had become on software.
events.funFact: Some people stocked canned food for Y2K—like getting ready for a storm that might never arrive.

events.description: Scientists announced a major draft of the human genome, accelerating biology and medicine. It changed how researchers study disease and inheritance.
events.funFact: Humans share about 99.9% of their DNA—meaning tiny differences help create all our variety.
